old_claim.note: Fire Control is an earlier historical predecessor or foundation, not a one-to-one engineering dependency.

new_claim.note: The documented objects are lamps because they held combustible fat and produced soot; controlled ignition and a sustained flame are therefore part of the evidenced operation.

source_shape.source_locator: Lighting section, paragraphs 2 and 4: identifies soot and animal-fat combustible in a recognized sandstone lamp and describes Lascaux fuel-holding stone lamps.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: Combustible fuel and soot identify the stone objects as flame-producing lamps.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: Unlike a contextual predecessor, fire is the operating process that turns the shaped fuel vessel into a source of light.

ontology_before: Fire was only an earlier historical backdrop to the generic lamp node.
ontology_after: Controlled combustion is a constitutive operating requirement of the source-pinned fat lamp.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed: the oil_lamps to fire_control edge is now required and directly evidenced.
would_reject_if: The objects were shown to provide light through a non-combustion mechanism or the soot and fuel interpretation was withdrawn.
short_reason: A fat lamp functions through controlled combustion.
